people.name: The Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad)
people.bio:
The Báb (meaning 'The Gate') was the Prophet-Herald of the Bahá'í Revelation. Born in Shiraz as Siyyid 'Alí-Muhammad, He declared His mission in 1844, initiating a spiritual revolution that shook 19th-century Persia. He called for moral regeneration and prepared His followers for the imminent arrival of 'Him Whom God shall make manifest' (Bahá'u'lláh).

His teachings spread rapidly, provoking intense opposition from the Islamic clergy and the royal court. He was imprisoned in the remote mountain fortresses of Máh-Kú and Chihríq, and was ultimately martyred by a firing squad of 750 soldiers in Tabriz on July 9, 1850.
people.confusionAlert: Sometimes referred to by His birth name Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad. Do not confuse with other individuals named Mírzá 'Alí or Muhammad.

people.name: Bahá'u'lláh (Mírzá Husayn-'Alí)
people.bio:
Bahá'u'lláh (born Mírzá Husayn-'Alí in Tehran) was a nobleman who rejected a life of courtly luxury to champion the Báb's cause. He became one of the most prominent Bábís, organizing the historic Conference of Badasht where the old laws of Islam were formally abrogated. He suffered imprisonment, torture, and the loss of all His properties following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, during which He was cast into the infamous subterranean prison, the Siyáh-Chál.

It was in the darkness of the Siyáh-Chál that He received the first intimations of His own divine mission. He is the Promised One foretold by the Báb and all previous scriptures, whose teachings form the foundation of the Bahá'í Faith.
people.confusionAlert: In Nabíl's Narrative, He is primarily referred to by His birth name Mírzá Husayn-'Alí or Jináb-i-Bahá. Do not confuse with Mullá Husayn.

people.name: Mullá Husayn-i-Bushrú'í

relations.type: First Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá Husayn was the first Letter of the Living and the initial believer in the Báb. A brilliant young scholar under Siyyid Kázim, he spent forty days in prayer and fasting before traveling to Shiraz, where on May 23, 1844, he recognized the Báb's station. For this, he was given the title 'Bábu'l-Báb' (the Gate of the Gate).

He was a figure of legendary courage and physical strength. He walked on foot across Persia to spread the new teachings, and later raised the Black Standard of Khurasan, marching with a band of Bábís to the forests of Mazandaran. He oversaw the construction of Fort Shaykh Tabarsí and led the defense against royal armies, performing feats of defense before being martyred by a bullet during a night sortie in early 1849.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Bahá'u'lláh (Mírzá Husayn-'Alí) or the Imam Husayn (the Shia martyr whose shrine is in Karbila).

people.name: Quddús (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Bárfurúshí)

relations.type: Last Letter of the Living, Companion on Pilgrimage

people.bio:
Quddús was the 18th and final Letter of the Living, considered by the Báb to have the highest spiritual rank among His disciples. Born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí in Mazandaran, he accompanied the Báb on His pilgrimage to Mecca and Medina, where he courageously assisted in proclaiming the Faith. Upon returning, he was arrested and tortured in Shiraz.

He was the spiritual leader of the Bábís at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí, where his presence inspired the defenders to withstand armies for months. After the fall of the fort due to a treacherous peace treaty, Quddús was delivered to the hostile clergy of Bárfurúsh, who tortured him brutally and martyred him in the public square in May 1849.
people.confusionAlert: His birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. Do not confuse with Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zanjání (Hujjat) or Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí (Anís).

people.name: Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí)
people.bio:
Vahíd (born Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí) was one of the most learned religious figures in Persia. He possessed such a prodigious memory that he had memorized over 30,000 Islamic traditions. Muhammad Shah sent him to investigate the Báb's claims, expecting him to disprove them. Instead, during their interviews in Shiraz, Vahíd was overwhelmed by the Báb's divine knowledge and became a devoted follower.

He traveled across Persia, converting thousands. When persecutions intensified, he led the Bábí defense during the Nayríz Upheaval, holding out in the fort of Khájih. He was martyred in 1850 after the townspeople violated an oath sworn on the Qur'án to grant the Bábís peace.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal), Bahá'u'lláh's half-brother.

people.name: Hujjat (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zanjání)
people.bio:
Hujjat (born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí) was an influential and independent-minded Mujtahid of Zanjan. Known for his theological brilliance and refusal to follow blind religious conformity, he accepted the Báb after receiving a written text from Him through a messenger.

His conversion prompted nearly his entire congregation to become Bábís, which enraged the local clergy. The resulting tensions erupted into the Zanján Upheaval (1850–1851), where Hujjat led several thousand Bábís (including women and children) in defending their quarter against imperial troops. Hujjat was wounded by a cannonball and died shortly before the fort fell. His wife and children were also martyred.
people.confusionAlert: His birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. Do not confuse with Quddús or Anís.

people.name: Táhirih (Qurratu'l-'Ayn)

relations.type: Protected by and closely allied with

people.bio:
Táhirih (born Fátimih Baraghání) was a poet, theologian, and the only female Letter of the Living. Her intellect was so formidable that she was permitted to attend the lectures of Siyyid Kázim behind a curtain and debate male scholars. She recognized the Báb through a dream and letter, and was accepted as a disciple without meeting Him in person.

She is famous for her role at the Conference of Badasht, where she appeared in public without a veil, symbolizing the separation of the Bábí Dispensation from Islamic tradition. After years of house arrest, she was choked to death with a silk scarf in a Tehran garden in August 1852. Her famous final words were: 'You can kill me as soon as you like, but you cannot stop the emancipation of women.'
people.confusionAlert: Sometimes referred to as Qurratu'l-'Ayn (given by Siyyid Kázim) or Táhirih (given by Bahá'u'lláh at Badasht).

people.name: Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í
people.bio:
Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í was the founder of the Shaykhí school of Shia Islam. He arose in the early 19th century to protest the corruption of the clergy and teach that the arrival of the Promised Mihdí (the Báb) and the Return of Christ (Bahá'u'lláh) were imminent. He explained Shia prophecies symbolically, which drew both massive devotion and accusations of heresy from orthodox Mujtahids.

He spent his final years traveling through Persia and Iraq, sowing the seeds of expectation. He instructed his students, most notably Siyyid Kázim, to disperse and search for the Promised One upon his death. He died in 1826 while on a pilgrimage to Mecca.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Shaykh Ahmad. Do not confuse with Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib.

people.name: Siyyid Kázim-i-Rashtí
people.bio:
Siyyid Kázim-i-Rashtí was the designated successor of Shaykh Ahmad. He led the Shaykhí community from Karbilá, defending the teachings against orthodox attacks. He focused intensely on preparing his disciples to recognize the Twin Revelations, telling them that the Promised One was already alive among them.

He refused to name a successor, telling his students that upon his death they must leave their books, homes, and studies, and wander the earth to search for the Mihdí. He died in late 1843, shortly before the Báb declared His mission in May 1844.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Siyyid Kázim. Do not confuse with other individuals named Kázim.

people.name: Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal)

relations.type: Nominal head of Bábís appointed by

people.bio:
Mírzá Yahyá was the younger half-brother of Bahá'u'lláh. Following the martyrdom of the Báb, he was appointed as the nominal head of the Bábí community as a protective measure to shield Bahá'u'lláh from state scrutiny. However, he spent most of his time in hiding and disguise, refusing to lead in times of danger.

Following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, he fled to Baghdad in disguise. He later opposed Bahá'u'lláh when He publicly declared His mission, claiming leadership for himself and creating a schism among the Bábís. He is remembered as the primary Covenant-breaker of the Báb Dispensation.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí) or Yahyá Khán (the warden of Chihríq).

people.name: Mírzá Músá (Áqáy-i-Kalím)
people.bio:
Mírzá Músá, commonly known as Áqáy-i-Kalím, was one of the two faithful brothers of Bahá'u'lláh (the other being Mírzá Mihdí, who died young). He accepted the Báb's cause early on and stood resolutely beside Bahá'u'lláh through all His exiles, imprisonments, and hardships.

He was recognized by Shoghi Effendi as one of the Apostles of Bahá'u'lláh, serving as a trusted pillar of the community and assisting in managing the affairs of the exiles in Baghdad, Adrianople, and Acre.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Kalím in the narrative. Do not confuse with other Mirzas.

people.name: Hájí Mírzá Áqásí

relations.type: Prime Minister and spiritual tutor of

people.bio:
Hájí Mírzá Áqásí was the Grand Vizier (Prime Minister) of Persia during the reign of Muhammad Shah. He exerted immense, almost hypnotic spiritual and political influence over the weak-willed Shah. Fearing that the Báb's growing popularity would strip him of his power, he actively blocked the Báb from meeting the Shah.

He ordered the Báb's confinement in the remote mountain fortresses of Máh-Kú and Chihríq, attempting to isolate Him from His followers. After the death of Muhammad Shah in 1848, he fell from power, was pillaged by his enemies, and fled to Iraq, where he died in obscurity.
people.confusionAlert: Often referred to simply as Áqásí or the Grand Vizier. Do not confuse with other Hajis.

people.name: Muhammad Sháh

relations.type: Sovereign who ordered the arrest of

people.bio:
Muhammad Sháh was the king (Sháh) of Persia from 1834 to 1848. A weak ruler plagued by illness, he fell entirely under the sway of his Grand Vizier, Hájí Mírzá Áqásí. Intrigued by the reports of the Báb, he sent Siyyid Yahyá-i-Dárábí (Vahíd) to investigate Him.

Although Vahíd reported back that the Báb was the promised Savior, the Shah was persuaded by the Grand Vizier to avoid meeting Him and instead order His confinement in Máh-Kú. Muhammad Shah died of gout in September 1848, sparking civil unrest and immediate upheavals across Persia.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with the Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad) or other figures named Muhammad.

people.name: Násiri'd-Dín Sháh
people.bio:
Násiri'd-Dín Sháh was the Crown Prince (Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá) who ascended the throne of Persia in 1848 after his father's death, reigning until his assassination in 1896. During the Báb's trial in Tabriz in 1848, the young prince presided over the assembly where the Báb declared His station to the assembled clergy.

Upon becoming Shah, under the guidance of his prime minister Amír Kabír, he ordered the execution of the Báb in 1850 and oversaw the brutal persecution of thousands of Bábís, culminating in the horrific massacres of 1852 following the failed assassination attempt on his life.
people.confusionAlert: Called Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá when he was the Crown Prince. Do not confuse with his father Muhammad Shah.

people.name: Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí
titles: First Martyr of the Bábí Dispensation

relations.type: Second Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí was the second Letter of the Living and the first martyr of the Bábí Dispensation. He accepted the Báb immediately after Mullá Husayn. The Báb sent him to Iraq to announce the declaration to the leading Shia clerics of Karbilá and Najaf.

His bold proclamations provoked a massive backlash from both Shia and Sunni authorities. He was arrested, tried in Baghdad in a famous inter-sectarian trial, and sent to Istanbul as a prisoner, where he was condemned to hard labor. He died in captivity around 1846, becoming the first to lay down his life for the Báb's cause.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí or other individuals named 'Alí.

people.name: Anís (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí)
people.bio:
Anís (born Mullá Muhammad-'Alí-i-Zunúzí) was a young Bábí from Tabriz who achieved eternal fame for his absolute devotion to the Báb. When the Báb was brought to Tabriz for execution, Anís threw himself at the Báb's feet, pleading to be allowed to share His martyrdom.

On July 9, 1850, he was suspended alongside the Báb in the public square. When the first regiment of 750 soldiers fired, the smoke cleared to reveal the Báb unharmed and the ropes severed, but Anís stood there untouched, refusing to leave the Báb's side. He was suspended again and shot by the second regiment, his body mingled with the Báb's in death.
people.confusionAlert: Birth name was Mullá Muhammad-'Alí. The name Anís (meaning 'Companion') was given to him by the Báb. Do not confuse with Quddús or Hujjat.

people.name: Hájí Siyyid Javád-i-Karbilá'í
people.bio:
Hájí Siyyid Javád-i-Karbilá'í was a revered and elder scholar of the Shaykhí school who had been a close associate of both Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kázim. He recognized the Báb's station immediately after His declaration.

He was renowned for his saintly life and traveled extensively, helping to confirm many prominent figures (including Vahíd) in their faith. He survived the early upheavals and later became a devoted follower of Bahá'u'lláh.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Siyyids or Mirzas.

people.name: Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí
people.bio:
Siyyid 'Alí-i-Zunúzí was a prominent and wealthy merchant of Tabriz who accepted the Báb. When his step-son Anís (Mullá Muhammad-'Alí) became obsessed with the Báb, Siyyid 'Alí tried to protect him by locking him in his room to prevent his arrest.

Despite his efforts, Anís escaped to join the Báb during His final days in Tabriz. Siyyid 'Alí is remembered as a devoted and sorrowful figure who witnessed the martyrdom of his step-son.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with the Báb (Mírzá 'Alí-Muhammad) or Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí.

people.name: Mullá Yusúf-i-Ardibilí

relations.type: Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio:
Mullá Yusúf-i-Ardibilí was a Letter of the Living. He was a learned divine who joined the Bábís at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí, participating actively in the defense and proving himself to be a capable and spiritual soldier.

He was captured after the fall of the fort and was martyred alongside the other survivors by the royal troops in Mazandaran in 1849.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Mullá Mihdíy-i-Yazdí
people.bio:
Mullá Mihdíy-i-Yazdí was a prominent Bábí of Yazd. When Vahíd arrived in Yazd to proclaim the Báb's message, Mullá Mihdí hosted him in his home and became one of his most active supporters.

He participated in defending Vahíd when local mobs and government forces attacked them, and was eventually arrested and martyred during the persecutions that swept Yazd.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other figures named Mihdí.

people.name: Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib
people.bio:
Mírzá Ahmad-i-Kátib (also known as Mírzá Ahmad-i-Qazvíní) was the close amanuensis (secretary) of the Báb. He transcribed many of the Báb's writings during His imprisonment and helped copy and distribute them to the believers.

He was highly regarded for his beautiful calligraphy and deep devotion. He traveled between Shiraz, Tehran, and the northern fortresses to convey the Báb's messages. He was martyred during the massacres of 1852 in Tehran.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsá'í.

people.name: Hájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí (Khál-i-A'zam)
people.bio:
Hájí Mírzá Siyyid 'Alí, known as Khál-i-A'zam, was the maternal uncle of the Báb who raised Him after His father died. He was a respected merchant in Shiraz who initially protected the Báb out of family affection and later became a devoted believer.

He refused to recant his faith when arrested, choosing instead to share the fate of his Nephew. He was martyred in Tehran in 1850 as one of the Seven Martyrs of Tehran.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly referred to as Khál-i-A'zam. Do not confuse with other Siyyids.

people.name: Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas (Jináb-i-Muqaddas)

relations.type: Early believer and victim of scourging under

people.bio:
Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas was a prominent scholar who accepted the Báb. He was the first Bábí to add the name of the Báb to the traditional call to prayer (Adhán) in Shiraz, which led to his immediate arrest, scourging, and expulsion from the city.

He survived the early persecutions and was titled 'Muqaddas' (The Holy) by Bahá'u'lláh for his absolute devotion and service to the Faith.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Jináb-i-Muqaddas. Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Siyyid Basír-i-Hindí

relations.type: Believer who recognized Him by voice

people.bio:
Siyyid Basír-i-Hindí was a blind Indian nobleman and spiritual seeker of Shia descent. He traveled to Persia and recognized the Báb's station immediately upon hearing His voice. Despite his blindness, he possessed a profound spiritual sight and courage.

He traveled across Persia, proclaiming the Báb's message. He was arrested, tortured with extreme cruelty, and martyred in Tehran around 1851.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly called Siyyid Basír. Do not confuse with other Shia Siyyids.

people.name: Mullá Báqir-i-Tabrízí

relations.type: Letter of the Living, Disciple of

people.bio: Mullá Báqir-i-Tabrízí was a Letter of the Living. He was the only Letter of the Living who survived all the Bábí upheavals and lived to recognize Bahá'u'lláh's station in Baghdad, remaining a faithful follower until his death.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Sulaymán Khán
people.bio:
Sulaymán Khán was a nobleman and military official who became a devoted Bábí. He is famous for the extraordinary manner of his martyrdom in Tehran in August 1852. The executioners cut holes in his chest and shoulders and inserted burning candles into his wounds.

He walked to the place of execution singing verses of joy, blowing out the candles himself as they burned down. His martyrdom is recorded as one of the most heroic and moving events in early Bábí history.
people.confusionAlert: Mainly called Sulaymán Khán. Do not confuse with other Khans.

people.name: Mullá Sálih-i-Baraghání
people.bio:
Mullá Sálih-i-Baraghání was a prominent and highly respected Mujtahid of Qazvín and the father of Táhirih. He loved his daughter deeply and educated her in theology and jurisprudence, but was deeply troubled by her conversion to the Bábí Faith.

He spent his final years in grief, torn between his love for his daughter and his orthodox religious obligations.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with his brother Mullá Muhammad-Taqí.

people.name: Mullá Muhammad-Taqí-i-Baraghání
people.bio:
Mullá Muhammad-Taqí-i-Baraghání (known in Bahá'í history as the Third Martyr) was a fanatical Shia Mujtahid of Qazvín who vehemently denounced Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kázim, and later the Báb.

His denouncements provoked a young Bábí to attack and kill him while he was praying in the mosque in 1847. His murder sparked a wave of arrests and persecutions of Bábís across Qazvín and Tehran, including the imprisonment of Táhirih.
people.confusionAlert: Often called Mullá Taqí. Do not confuse with his brother Mullá Sálih.

people.name: Mullá ''Abdu'r-Rahím
people.bio: Mullá 'Abdu'r-Rahím was the chief Mujtahid of Zanján who led the orthodox opposition against Hujjat. He wrote numerous letters to the Shah and the Grand Vizier denouncing Hujjat as a heretic, which ultimately led to Hujjat's arrest and the subsequent Zanján Upheaval.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Mullas.

people.name: Hamzih Mírzá
people.bio:
Hamzih Mírzá, titled Hishmatu'd-Dawlih, was a brother of Muhammad Shah and a prominent prince of the royal Qajar house. He was appointed as the military commander of the imperial forces sent to crush the Bábí defenders at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí.

He suffered several humiliating defeats at the hands of Mullá Husayn's small band of defenders before ultimately besieging the fort and capturing it through a treacherous oath of peace.
people.confusionAlert: Often referred to as Hishmatu'd-Dawlih. Do not confuse with other Mirzas.

people.name: 'Alí Khán of Máh-Kú
people.bio:
'Alí Khán was the warden of the mountain fortress of Máh-Kú where the Báb was imprisoned. Initially hostile and strict, he was deeply affected by the Báb's saintly character, His nightly prayers, and the devotion of His followers.

He eventually relaxed the prison conditions, allowing pilgrims to visit the Báb, and became a secret believer, refusing to carry out the harsher orders of the Grand Vizier.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with other Khans or individuals named 'Alí.

people.name: Yahyá Khán
people.bio: Yahyá Khán was the warden of the fortress of Chihríq and the brother-in-law of the Grand Vizier, Hájí Mírzá Áqásí. Although he was expected to enforce strict confinement on the Báb, he also fell under the spell of the Báb's character and treated Him with kindness and respect.
people.confusionAlert: Do not confuse with Vahíd (Siyyid Yahyá) or Mírzá Yahyá (Subh-i-Azal).

people.name: 'Ábidín-i-Shahmirzadi
people.bio: Mulla Zaynu'l-'Abidin-i-Shahmirzadi was a trusted and learned counsellor of the government. In Chapter 20, he acquainted a prince with the story and activities of Siyyid Ahmad and the two sons of Karbila'i Abu-Muhammad.

people.name: 'Ábidín-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Mulla Zaynu'l-'Abidin-i-Yazdi was a martyr of the Faith mentioned in Chapter 26. His martyrdom was followed by rumors that the government intended to put other prominent believers, such as Tahirih and Siyyid Husayn, to death.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Azghandi
people.bio: Mirza Ahmad-i-Azghandi was the first to embrace the Faith in Khurasan and was known as the most learned and wise of the province's ulamas. He displayed conspicuous early zeal and later steadfastly refused to believe rumors of the Bab's death.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Bahrayni
people.bio: Shaykh Ahmad-i-Bahrayni was a deceased religious figure mentioned in Chapter 15. The narrator describes a confrontation with a critic who disparaged the deity Shaykh Ahmad worshipped and characterized his followers as embodiments of error.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Ibdal
people.bio: Mulla Ahmad-i-Ibdal-i-Maraghi'i was one of the early disciples of the Bab mentioned in Chapter 3. In Chapter 16, while at Badasht, a message from Mirza Sulayman was sent to him regarding an unrecognized spiritual radiance.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Mu'allim
people.bio: Mulla Ahmad-i-Mu'allim was a Shaykhi from Khurasan who previously served as the instructor of Siyyid Kazim's children in Karbila. He was the second person in his province to embrace the Faith of the Bab.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Nukhud-Biriz
people.bio: Mir Ahmad-i-Nukhud-Biriz is a figure mentioned in Chapter 22. His son, Abu-Talib, is listed among the companions participating in the narrative's events.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Saffar
people.bio: Mulla Ahmad-i-Saffar is a believer mentioned in Chapter 20 in a list of companions associated with the events of the narrative.

people.name: Ahmad-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Siyyid Ahmad-i-Yazdi was the father of Siyyid Husayn, the Bab's amanuensis. In Chapter 21, he suddenly appeared at a royal wedding in Tihran to deliver an important message to Baha'u'llah.

people.name: Akbar-i-Ardistani
people.bio: Mulla Ali-Akbar-i-Ardistani was one of the first believers to suffer persecution on Persian soil for the Cause of God. He studied under Siyyid Kazim for four years and recognized the Bab in Shiraz at the age of twenty-two.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Ardistani
people.bio: Mirza Haydar-'Aliy-i-Ardistani was a believer who prepared a brief survey of the struggle in Zanjan. His survey served as a key source of information for the narrator's account.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Banna
people.bio: Ustad Qurban-'Aliy-i-Banna is a figure mentioned in Chapter 20. His son Abdu'llah and younger brother Ustad Ja'far are also noted in connection with the narrative.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Haddad
people.bio: Ustad Mihr-'Aliy-i-Haddad was a survivor of the massacre of Zanjan. As a child under ten, he was part of a nine-member children's delegation sent by Hujjat to the enemy camp.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Harati
people.bio: Mulla Abdu'l-'Aliy-i-Harati was an associate who feigned submission to the Revelation of the Bab out of ambition for leadership. He secretly worked to undermine Mulla Husayn and eventually demonstrated a base character.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Mudhahhib
people.bio: Mulla Aliy-i-Mudhahhib was one of the five attendants who accompanied a leader to the camp of the enemy in Chapter 22.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Mutavalli
people.bio: Mulla Nad-'Aliy-i-Mutavalli was one of five ascertained believers from Savad-Kuh who participated in the events described in Chapter 20.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Nahri
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-'Aliy-i-Nahri was a prominent Siyyid of Isfahan who recognized the truth of the Cause. He hosted early meetings in his home, and his daughter later married the Most Great Branch.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-'Aliy-i-Qazvini was one of the Letters of the Living who personally met the Bab. In Chapter 16, he accompanied Quddus from Mashhad to Mazindaran.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Sardar
people.bio: Mirza Ali, surnamed Aliy-i-Sardar for his exceptional courage, was a young man who extended great solicitude to the survivors of the struggle following Vahid's death. He is also mentioned in Chapter 22, where his uncle Mirza Ahmad was placed in charge of a mill tower near the fort.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Tabib
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-'Aliy-i-Tabib-i-Zanjani was a devoted martyr from Zanjan whose faith remained unshaken by persecution. His first-hand accounts of the Zanjan struggle served as a primary source for the narrator.

people.name: 'Alíy-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-'Aliy-i-Zanjani was a believer who delivered a Tablet to Grand Vazir Haji Mirza Aqasi. He later visited Baha'u'llah in Tihran in the company of Mirza Masih-i-Nuri.

people.name: Áqáy-i-Rikab-Saz
people.bio: Mirza Aqay-i-Rikab-Saz was a follower of the Bab in Shiraz who became deeply enamored of Him. His convictions and love remained steadfast despite severe and prolonged persecution.

people.name: Ard-i-Jannat
people.bio: Ard-i-Jannat (the Land of Paradise) is a term used in Chapter 13 to refer to Milan, from which luscious fruits were plucked and consecrated for a feast by Muhammad-Taqi.

people.name: 'Azím-i-Khu
people.bio: Siyyid Abdu'l-'Azim-i-Khu'i, surnamed the Siyyid-i-Khal-Dar, was a defender of the fort of Tabarsi noted for his dignified bearing. He served as a messenger for Quddus and was later summoned by Vahid to ride through Yazd during an upheaval.

people.name: Báf-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Hasan-i-Sha'r-Baf-i-Yazdi is a believer mentioned in Chapter 20 in a list of companions associated with the events of the narrative.

people.name: Bágh-i-Jannat
people.bio: Bagh-i-Jannat was an orchard and house named by Baha'u'llah after Tahirih's safe departure from Tihran. Tahirih stayed in this spot for seven days to entertain other believers.

people.name: Bágh-i-Takht
people.bio: Bagh-i-Takht was a location mentioned in Chapter 9 to which a master and his family fled during a time of severe illness, leaving their dead unburied.

people.name: Bakhsh-i-Qúchání
people.bio: Mulla Khuda-Bakhsh-i-Quchani, who was later named Mulla Ali, was one of the early believers of the Bab mentioned in Chapter 3.

people.name: Band-i-Isfahání
people.bio: Siyyid Abu'l-Qasim-i-'Alaqih-Band-i-Isfahani was a companion of Mirza Ahmad on his journey to Kirmanshah. In Chapter 25, he was instructed by Azim to guide the narrator to the gate of Qum.

people.name: Báqir-i-Bastámí
people.bio: Mulla Baqir-i-Bastami was a believer in Tihran whose room at the Madrisiy-i-Sadr was used by Baha'u'llah, as described in Chapter 21.

people.name: Báqir-i-Kafsh-Dúz
people.bio: Karbila'i Baqir-i-Kafsh-Duz is listed in Chapter 22 as one of the companions present during the events described in the narrative.

people.name: Báqir-i-Kandí
people.bio: Mulla Baqir-i-Kandi was an occupant of the fort of Tabarsi alongside his brother Mulla Mihdi. In Chapter 20, an acquaintance sought permission to enter the fort to persuade them to escape the imminent danger.

people.name: Báqir-i-Qá'iní
people.bio: Mulla Mirza Muhammad-Baqir-i-Qa'ini was a devoted believer residing in Mashhad who built the Babiyyih in the Bala-Khiyaban neighborhood. In Chapter 16, Quddus enjoined him and other prominent companions to bear unquestioned allegiance to Mulla Husayn.

people.name: Báqir-i-Sabbágh
people.bio: Mashhadi Baqir-i-Sabbagh is mentioned in Chapter 22 in relation to his son, Zaynu'l-'Abidin, who was listed among the companions in the narrative.

people.name: Bavanát-i-Fárs
people.bio: Bavanat-i-Fars is a mountainous district where Vahid took refuge. Most of its inhabitants, including the Shaykhu'l-Islam Haji Siyyid Isma'il, readily embraced the Cause.

people.name: Big-i-Chápárchí
people.bio: Muhammad Big-i-Chaparchi was a mounted escort leader of the Aliyu'llahi sect who helped conduct the Bab in disguise. He hosted the Bab's uncle Siyyid Ali, and later argued that the captive Bab should lodge in a caravanserai in Zanjan.

people.name: Big-i-Sanandají
people.bio: Mustafa Big-i-Sanandaji, a dervish surnamed Majdhub, composed a widely circulated poem in praise of Baha'u'llah without premeditation or initially knowing the identity of his Beloved.

people.name: Buzurg-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Buzurg-i-Nuri was a renowned noble from Mazindaran celebrated for his character, charm, and artistic and intellectual attainments. In Chapter 4, Mulla Husayn enquired whether any family member was capable of maintaining his high traditions.

people.name: Dawlíy-i-Isfahání
people.bio: Sadru'd-Dawliy-i-Isfahani was a general of the imperial army who led two regiments. In Chapter 24, he received orders from the Grand Vazir to cancel his journey to Adhirbayjan and proceed immediately to Zanjan.

people.name: Dín-i-Malikí-Dúz
people.bio: Karbila'i Shamsu'd-Din-i-Maliki-Duz is a figure mentioned in Chapter 22. His son, Karbila'i Hasan, is listed among the companions in the narrative.

people.name: Dín-i-Naráqí
people.bio: Haji Mirza Kamalu'd-Din-i-Naraqi was a believer who announced Baha'u'llah's Revelation in Baghdad to the narrator. He was a descendant of Haji Mulla Mihdi, the author of the *Muhriqu'l-Qulub*.

people.name: Dín-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Haji Nasiru'd-Din-i-Qazvini was a companion at the fort of Tabarsi who set aside a cow to prepare a daily pudding for Quddus.

people.name: Fawj-i-Kalbát
people.bio: The Fawj-i-Kalbat was an imperial regiment whose commander (Sartip) is mentioned in Chapter 20 in connection with the siege of the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Hádíy-i-Farhádí
people.bio: Muhammad-Hadiy-i-Farhadi was a believer summoned and entrusted by Baha'u'llah with the task of delivering Tahirih from her strict confinement in Qazvin and escorting her to Tihran.

people.name: Hakkák-i-Qumí
people.bio: Fathu'llah-i-Hakkak-i-Qumi was a companion who attempted an assault on the Shah, inflicting only slight wounds. In Chapter 26, he is described as being subjected to inhuman treatment following the attempt.

people.name: Haram-i-Ma'súmih
people.bio: Haram-i-Ma'sumih is a holy shrine and inviolable sanctuary in Qum where criminals were immune from arrest. In Chapter 12, the Bab's guards offered to ignore government orders to let Him visit it, but He declined.

people.name: Hasan-i-Bájistání
people.bio: Mulla Hasan-i-Bajistani was one of the early believers of the Bab mentioned in Chapter 3.

people.name: Hasan-i-Bazzáz
people.bio: Haji Abu'l-Hasan-i-Bazzaz was a fellow traveler of the Bab during His pilgrimage to the Hijaz. Initially having only a dim recognition of the Bab's mission, he was later profoundly transformed in Shiraz.

people.name: Hasan-i-Chít-Saz
people.bio: Muhammad-Hasan-i-Chit-Saz was a well-known cloth manufacturer who attained the presence of the Bab, as mentioned in Chapter 20.

people.name: Hasan-i-Gawhar
people.bio: Mirza Hasan-i-Gawhar was a prominent, exceptionally corpulent disciple of Siyyid Kazim. In Chapter 3, Mulla Husayn fearlessly delivered their late leader's parting message to him, urging him to seek the promised one.

people.name: Hasan-i-Najafí
people.bio: Shaykh Muhammad-Hasan-i-Najafi was a preeminent mujtahid of Najaf. In Chapter 20, a seeker recounts that Shaykh Muhammad-Hasan-i-Najafi angrily rebuked him and refused multiple requests to expound upon the principles of Islam.

people.name: Hasan-i-Náyiní
people.bio: Haji Hasan-i-Nayini was an intimate friend of Abdu'l-Vahhab, who confided in him the secret entrusted by his master. He is mentioned in Chapter 1 in relation to a date he provided that perplexed the narrator.

people.name: Hasan-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Hasan-i-Nuri was a noted Platonist who attended a meeting with the Bab at the home of the Mu'tamid of Isfahan. During the gathering, he requested the Bab to elucidate certain philosophical or spiritual questions.

people.name: Hasan-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Muhammad-Hasan-i-Qazvini, also known as Fata or Fata'l-Qazvini, was a messenger for Tahirih who accompanied her on her journey toward Khurasan. He also conveyed a pressing invitation from Tahirih to Quddus.

people.name: Hasan-i-Rashtí
people.bio: Mulla Hasan-i-Rashti is mentioned in the Chapter 20 narrative alongside Siyyid Husayn-i-Kulah-Duz and Mulla Ni'matu'llah-i-Barfurushi. The context lists him in relation to events in Barfurush.

people.name: Hasan-i-Shá'ir
people.bio: Hasan-i-Sha'r-Baf-i-Yazdi is mentioned in the Chapter 20 narrative alongside other figures such as Mulla Ahmad-i-Saffar and Mulla Husayn-i-Miskar.

people.name: Hasan-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Haji Abu'l-Hasan-i-Shirazi traveled in the same vessel as the Bab during a memorable voyage lasting approximately two months. He later provided a detailed description of the circumstances of that journey.

people.name: Hujjat-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-'Ali of Zanjan, surnamed Hujjat-i-Zanjani by the Bab, was a formidable champion of the Cause and an ecclesiastical dignitary of his age. Known for his independent mind and originality, he denounced the traditional ecclesiastical hierarchy of his country.

people.name: Husayn-i-Hakamíy
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-Husayn-i-Hakamiy-i-Kirmani was one of the believers present in Chapter 19 when Mirza Ahmad related the circumstances of a specific incident. He is mentioned alongside figures like Haji Mulla Isma'il-i-Farahani and Mirza Habibu'llah-i-Isfahani.

people.name: Husayn-i-Káshí-Saz
people.bio: Mirza Husayn-i-Kashi-Saz is mentioned in Chapter 22 of the narrative as the father of Mirza Ahmad. He appears in a list of individuals including Karbila'i Mirza Muhammad-i-Zari' and Karbila'i Baqir-i-Kafsh-Duz.

people.name: Husayn-i-Kirmání
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-Husayn-i-Kirmani was a well-known lecturer on the metaphysical doctrines of Islam who lodged in the Daru'sh-Shafa' madrisih. In Chapter 21, he is described as attempting multiple times to induce Siyyid Muhammad to take action.

people.name: Husayn-i-Kulah-Dúz
people.bio: Siyyid Husayn-i-Kulah-Duz was a resident of Barfurush who was martyred during the conflicts. His head was impaled on a lance and paraded through the town.

people.name: Husayn-i-Marághi'í
people.bio: Muhammad-Husayn-i-Maraghiyi was a captive who obstinately refused to speak despite being tortured and pressured. He later rushed forward to request immediate martyrdom before his companions were executed.

people.name: Husayn-i-Matavalliy
people.bio: Mirza Husayn-i-Matavalliy-i-Qumi was a prisoner who, unlike his companions who heroically chose to endure hunger, expressed a desire to eat the food spread before them by the sovereign.

people.name: Husayn-i-Miskár
people.bio: Mulla Husayn-i-Miskar is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative alongside other individuals such as Mulla Ahmad-i-Saffar and Hasan-i-Sha'r-Baf-i-Yazdi.

people.name: Husayn-i-Qannád
people.bio: Khajih Husayn-i-Qannad, the son of Khajih Ghani, is mentioned in Chapter 22 of the narrative alongside other individuals such as Ali, the son of Khayru'llah.

people.name: Husayn-i-Qutb
people.bio: Mirza Husayn-i-Qutb was a kad-khuda who is mentioned in Chapter 22 along with his relatives. He appears in the narrative alongside other figures such as Mulla Baqir, the Imam of the Chinar-Sukhtih quarter.

people.name: Husayn-i-Shushtarí
people.bio: Ghulam-Husayn-i-Shushtari was a merchant in Kirmanshah who was designated to conduct the narrator to the house where Mirza Ahmad was staying.

people.name: Husayn-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Mulla Husayn-i-Zanjani wrote a manuscript containing information about the events of a historical episode and sent it to Baha'u'llah. The narrator of Chapter 24 based part of their account on this manuscript.

people.name: Ibn-i-Muljam
people.bio: Ibn-i-Muljam was the assassin of Imam Ali. Imam Ali instructed his disciples that only his murderer alone should be put to death to atone for the act, sparing all others.

people.name: Ibn-i-Sa'd
people.bio: Ibn-i-Sa'd (referred to as Ibn-i-Sa) is mentioned in Chapter 23 as an ignoble historical figure associated with the slaying of an innocent descendant of the Prophet of God.

people.name: Ibn-i-Ziyád
people.bio: Ibn-i-Ziyad is referenced in Chapter 23 as an ignoble figure associated with the slaying of an innocent descendant of the Prophet of God.

people.name: Ibráhím-i-Gulpaygani
people.bio: Mulla Ibrahim-i-Gulpaygani was one of the two companions who remained with Tahirih. He subsequently suffered martyrdom in Qazvin.

people.name: Ibráhím-i-Kalbásí
people.bio: Haji Muhammad-Ibrahim-i-Kalbasi was a religious figure whose disciples referred a matter to him after failing to influence Haji Siyyid Asadu'llah. He is described in Chapter 4 as having fallen seriously ill and being on the verge of death.

people.name: Ibráhím-i-Mahallátí
people.bio: Mulla Ibrahim-i-Mahallati was a companion of a leader, highly esteemed for his learning and character. He was savagely put to death by enemies immediately after arriving at Qazvin.

people.name: Ibráhím-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Mirza Ibrahim-i-Shirazi was initially associated with a group of early believers, but in the course of his association, he gradually betrayed blindness of heart and demonstrated a base character.

people.name: Imárat-i-Khurshíd
people.bio: The Imarat-i-Khurshid was the private residence of the Mu'tamidu'd-Dawlih. It served as the place where the Bab resided as a guest and was hosted in Isfahan.

people.name: Jabal-i-Básit
people.bio: The Jabal-i-Basit is a location in the mountains of Khuy where the Bab predicted he would be confined for nine months before being transferred.

people.name: Jabal-i-Shadíd
people.bio: The Jabal-i-Shadid is a location in the mountains of Khuy containing the fortress of Chihriq. The Bab was incarcerated there, preventing him from personally assisting Quddus in his struggle.

people.name: Jalíl-i-Urúmí
people.bio: Mulla Jalil-i-Urumi, a native of Urumiyyih, was one of the Letters of the Living. He was highly noted for his learning, eloquence, and an exceptional tenacity of faith.

people.name: Jání-i-Káshání
people.bio: Haji Mirza Janiy-i-Kashani was one of the companions who accompanied Baha'u'llah on his journey from Nur to the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Javád-i-Baraghání
people.bio: Mulla Javad-i-Baraghani feigned submission to the Bab's Revelation in hope of leadership, but later betrayed his blindness of heart and demonstrated disloyalty. He also accompanied the narrator from Hamadan to Karbila.

people.name: Javád-i-Farhádí
people.bio: Muhammad-Javad-i-Farhadi is mentioned in the narrative of Qazvin as the son of Haji Allah-Vardi and the nephew of the martyred merchant Haji Asadu'llah.

people.name: Jazíriy-i-Khadrá
people.bio: The Jaziriy-i-Khadra is a destination where the Bab urged his followers, including Mulla Husayn and his companions, to march in order to assist Quddus.

people.name: Jináb-i-Muballigh
people.bio: Jinab-i-Muballigh was a believer from Shiraz who requested Baha'u'llah, during His early imprisonment in Akka, to explain the mysteries of a specific Tablet revealed by the Bab.

people.name: Karím-i-Íravání
people.bio: Mulla Abdu'l-Karim-i-Iravani was a highly respected divine of Qazvin, admired for his vast erudition, piety, and virtue, whose lectures the narrator faithfully attended.

people.name: Karím-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Mulla Abdu'l-Karim-i-Qazvini was an early believer who resided at the Nim-Avard madrisih. The Bab met with him, asked if he was seeking the Manifestation, and later sent him handwritten instructions.

people.name: Kháliq-i-Isfahání
people.bio: Abdu'l-Khaliq-i-Isfahani was a companion who was so shocked by Tahirih's sudden unveiling at Badasht that he cut his own throat in excitement and fled.

people.name: Kháliq-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Mulla Abdu'l-Khaliq-i-Yazdi was a prominent disciple of Shaykh Ahmad who was noted for his authority and learning. His son was also a highly praised youth who actively served the Cause.

people.name: Khan-i-Afghán
people.bio: Habibu'llah Khan-i-Afghan and Nuru'llah Khan-i-Afghan were officers who served under Abdu'llah Khan-i-Turkaman during the military operations against the defenders of the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Khan-i-Afshár
people.bio: Sulayman Khan-i-Afshar (also known as Sulayman Khan-i-Afshar-i-Shahriyari) was an admirer of Siyyid Kazim who failed to respond to the Bab's appeal for deliverance. He later commanded government forces against the defenders of the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Khan-i-Du-Dungí
people.bio: Ali-Asghar Khan-i-Du-Dungi'i is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative as one of the military officers involved in the operations against the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Khan-i-Íravání
people.bio: Husayn Khan-i-Iravani, surnamed Ajudan-Bashi and designated as Sahib-Ikhtiyar, was the governor of Fars who was ordered to investigate the sudden commotion caused by the Bab's followers.

people.name: Khan-i-Kalántar
people.bio: Mahmud Khan-i-Kalantar was a government official in Tihran in whose house Tahirih and fourteen other captured disciples of the Bab were imprisoned. He treated Tahirih with courtesy, but also ordered the arrest of other believers based on a provided list.

people.name: Khan-i-Karávulí
people.bio: Dhu'l-Faqar Khan-i-Karavuli is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative among the officers involved in the actions against the fort of Tabarsi.

people.name: Khan-i-Karrúsí
people.bio: Hasan-Ali Khan-i-Karrusi was a commander appointed to lead two Sunni regiments to Zanjan, whose arrival signaled the concentration of artillery bombardment on the fort.

people.name: Khan-i-Khamsih
people.bio: Aqa Jan Khan-i-Khamsih (also known as Nasiri), the colonel of the body-guard, volunteered to execute the Bab in Tabriz after Sam Khan's regiment refused to repeat the attempt.

people.name: Khan-i-Kurd
people.bio: Yahya Khan-i-Kurd was the warden of the castle of Chihriq, where the Bab was imprisoned under strict instructions from the Grand Vazir. His sister was the wife of Muhammad Shah.

people.name: Khan-i-Lálih-Báshí
people.bio: Rida-Quli Khan-i-Lalih-Bashi was a historian who, in his work 'Tarikh-i-Nasiri,' praised the prodigious strength and unrivalled skill of Mulla Husayn.

people.name: Khan-i-Láríjání
people.bio: Abbas-Quli Khan-i-Larijani was a military commander who marched against the fort of Tabarsi and swore an oath on the Qur'an to deceive the companions. He was also related to the local governor.

people.name: Khan-i-Máh-Kú
people.bio: Ali Khan-i-Mah-Ku'i was the warden of the castle of Mah-Ku who held the Bab in custody and was eventually won over to disregard strict government orders regarding the imprisonment.

people.name: Khan-i-Marághi'í
people.bio: Abdu'l-'Ali Khan-i-Maraghiyi was the captain of the prince's artillery who declared himself an admirer of Mulla Husayn and intervened to plead for his safety.

people.name: Khan-i-Mukrí
people.bio: Aziz Khan-i-Mukri, surnamed Sardar-i-Kull, was a military official who met with Hujjat in Zanjan at the house of Siyyid Ali Khan while on a mission to Iravan.

people.name: Khan-i-Námdár
people.bio: Ja'far-Quli Khan-i-Namdar was a commander who, alongside Amir Arslan Khan, conducted cruel military operations against the forces of the Shah.

people.name: Khan-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Aqa Khan-i-Nuri, known also as I'timadu'd-Dawlih, succeeded Haji Mirza Aqasi as Grand Vazir of the Shah. He envied Baha'u'llah's standing and was one of the first in Tihran to learn of the Bab's martyrdom.

people.name: Khan-i-Qájár
people.bio: Ibrahim Khan-i-Qajar-i-Kirmani is mentioned in Chapter 2 as the father of Haji Mirza Karim, who is described as being one-eyed and sparsely bearded.

people.name: Khan-i-Sardar
people.bio: The contexts mention Rida Khan-i-Sardar, who married the sister of the Sipah-Salar and provided refuge to a prisoner in his home before the struggle of Shaykh Tabarsi. They also mention Aziz Khan-i-Sardar, whose men demanded the delivery of Tahirih and who personally struck down Tahirih and dealt the fatal blow to the Bab's amanuensis.

people.name: Khan-i-Savád-Kúhí
people.bio: Khalil Khan-i-Savad-Kuhi is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative alongside other figures such as Khuda-Murad Khan-i-Kurd and Ja'far-Quli Khan-i-Surkh-Karri'i.

people.name: Khan-i-Sháh-Sún
people.bio: Husayn-'Ali Khan-i-Shah-Sun was the head of the Shah-Sun regiment, which was ordered to proceed to the village of Takur, where Baha'u'llah's home was located.

people.name: Khan-i-Surkh-Karrí
people.bio: Ja'far-Quli Khan-i-Surkh-Karri'i is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative alongside several other figures, including Khalil Khan-i-Savad-Kuhi and Khuda-Murad Khan-i-Kurd.

people.name: Khan-i-Turkamán
people.bio: Haji Mustafa Khan-i-Turkaman advised his sovereign to authorize his brother, Abdu'llah Khan-i-Turkaman, to subjugate a band of religious students. Abdu'llah Khan-i-Turkaman subsequently led a force against them alongside officers Habibu'llah Khan-i-Afghan and Nuru'llah Khan-i-Afghan.

people.name: Khan-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad Rawdih-Khan-i-Yazdi is listed in Chapter 3 of the narrative alongside other figures such as Siyyid Husayn-i-Yazdi, Mulla Hasan-i-Bajistani, and Mulla Mahmud-i-Khu'i.

people.name: Khání-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Aqa Khani-i-Nuri was the Minister of War who pleaded against putting to death a banished siyyid, arguing that it would be an act of manifest cruelty.

people.name: Khusraw-i-Qádí-Kalá
people.bio: Khusraw-i-Qadi-Kala'i was a leader of horsemen tasked with ensuring the safe passage of a party through Shir-Gah. He was later confidentially instructed by the Sa'idu'l-'Ulama' to confiscate the property of the party under his charge.

people.name: Khutbiy-i-Qahriyyih
people.bio: The Khutbiy-i-Qahriyyih is an epistle written in bold and moving language that denounces the character and actions of Haji Mirza Aqasi, the Grand Vazir of Muhammad Shah.

people.name: Kitab-i-Aqdas
people.bio: The Kitab-i-Aqdas is a work written by Baha'u'llah in which he alludes to symbols of a new cycle of Divine Revelation, including 'the mastery of the Great Reversal' and 'the Sign of the Sovereign.'

people.name: Kitab-i-Íqán
people.bio: The Kitab-i-Iqan is a book in which Baha'u'llah extols the virtues of Mulla Husayn and testifies to the great attainments and learning of Mulla Baqir and Mulla Mihdi.

people.name: Kitáb-i-Panj-Sha'n
people.bio: The Kitab-i-Panj-Sha'n is one of the last works of the Bab, in which he mentions the Naw-Ruz of the year 1266 A.H.

people.name: Kull-i-Shay'
people.bio: Kull-i-Shay' is a term referring to a complete numerical grouping of believers that includes the Bab's own name and the eighteen Letters of the Living.

people.name: Lawh-i-Hurúfát
people.bio: The Lawh-i-Hurufat is a Tablet revealed by the Bab in honor of Dayyan, which Dayyan praised as sufficient testimony to establish the Bab's truth.

people.name: Madrisiy-i-Dárú
people.bio: The Madrisiy-i-Daru'sh-Shafa' (or Daru'sh-Shafa'i-Masjid-i-Shah) was a school or lodging place where Siyyid Isma'il, Mulla Abdu'l-Karim, Mirza Muhammad-Husayn-i-Kirmani, and a siyyid from Kashan resided.

people.name: Madrisiy-i-Mírzá-Sálih
people.bio: The Madrisiy-i-Mirza-Salih is a school mentioned in Chapter 21 as a location to which a young child, referred to as the Most Great Branch, was conducted.

people.name: Madrisiy-i-Sadr
people.bio: The Madrisiy-i-Sadr is a school where Baha'u'llah stayed in a room occupied by Mulla Baqir-i-Bastami, and where a letter was delivered to him.

people.name: Mahmúd-i-Khu
people.bio: Mulla Mahmud-i-Khu'i was a distinguished divine and the brother of Mulla Mihdi, one of the Letters of the Living.

people.name: Mahmúd-i-Muqárí
people.bio: Mahmud-i-Muqari'i was a noted cloth dealer who was newly married when he attained the presence of the Bab in the castle of Chihriq and was urged to assist Quddus.

people.name: Mahmúd-i-Qamsarí
people.bio: Mirza Mahmud-i-Qamsari was an esteemed elder from Kashan who, at over ninety years of age, shared accounts highlighting the perspicacity of Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsa'i.

people.name: Majíd-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Haji Abdu'l-Majid-i-Shirazi is mentioned in Chapter 9, where his wife, alongside Haji Siyyid Javad-i-Karbila'i, was instructed by Baha'u'llah to teach the principles of the Faith to a woman.

people.name: Mamálik-i-Áshtiyání
people.bio: Mustawfiyu'l-Mamalik-i-Ashtiyani was a figure who was completely won over to the Faith after being presented with copies of a text on behalf of the author.

people.name: Masíh-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Masih-i-Nuri is mentioned in Chapter 18 as a believer who, along with others, was visited by Muhammad-'Aliy-i-Zanjani after the delivery of a Tablet to Haji Mirza Aqasi.

people.name: Masjid-i-Baráthá
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Baratha is a mosque situated on the highway between Baghdad and Kazimayn, where Siyyid Kazim and his companions stopped at the hour of noon to pray.

people.name: Masjid-i-Ílkhání
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Ilkhani is a mosque in Shiraz where the Bab instructed his companions to wait for him, and where he later joined them in prayer and teaching.

people.name: Masjid-i-Imám-Hasan
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Imam-Hasan is a mosque mentioned in Chapter 21 where an associate of the Faith performed his prayers and religious rites under observation.

people.name: Masjid-i-Jámi'
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Jami' is a mosque that a group of believers tried to reach swiftly while repulsing an attack with swords, rifles, sticks, and stones.

people.name: Masjid-i-Jum'ih
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Jum'ih is a mosque resembling the holy shrine of Mecca, where the voice of the Herald of a new Manifestation was destined to be proclaimed.

people.name: Masjid-i-Kázim-Big
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Kazim-Big is a mosque in whose neighborhood Haji Mulla Muhammad-i-Hamzih buried the bodies of slain believers and convinced their murderer to repent.

people.name: Masjid-i-Kúfih
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Kufih is a mosque where Mulla Husayn spent forty days in retirement, prayer, and contemplation, and where he was later joined by Mulla 'Aliy-i-Bastami.

people.name: Masjid-i-Naw
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Naw is a mosque where a prayer leader suddenly proclaimed the additional words prescribed by the Bab while sounding the adhan, causing astonishment and dismay among the congregation.

people.name: Masjid-i-Sháh
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Shah is a mosque in Tehran, located opposite a madrisih, which served as a meeting and rendezvous point for believers such as Siyyid Isma'il-i-Zavari'i and Mirza Ahmad.

people.name: Masjid-i-Vakíl
people.bio: The Masjid-i-Vakil is a mosque in Shiraz where the Bab was requested and summoned to appear on a Friday to publicly address the congregation and fulfill his pledge.

people.name: Mihdíy-i-Kandí
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-Mihdiy-i-Kandi was a close companion of Baha'u'llah and tutor to his household, who delivered a sealed letter and gifts to the Bab, and later left his infant son to join the defenders at Fort Tabarsi.

people.name: Mihdíy-i-Khu
people.bio: Mulla Mihdiy-i-Khu'i was a disciple of Siyyid Kazim who publicly declared him the Promised One, later traveled from Tehran to visit the Bab, and was eventually sent to Sari to demand the release of Quddus.

people.name: Mírzáy-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Shaykh Ali-Mirzay-i-Shirazi was a staunch supporter of the Cause who served it with selflessness and devotion until his last breath.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Akhbárí
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-i-Akhbari was a poet who made predictions regarding the year of the Manifestation (1260 A.H.) in his writings.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Furughi
people.bio: Mullá Mírzá Muhammad-i-Furughi was a prominent scholar from Khurasan who became a devoted Bábí. He was one of the survivors of the siege at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí, where he witnessed the heroic defense. Nabíl-i-A'zam's narrative relies heavily on Mirza Muhammad's memoirs and oral reports, citing him as a primary eyewitness for the events surrounding Fort Shaykh Tabarsí and the lives of early believers.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Hamzih
people.bio: Haji Mulla Muhammad-i-Hamzih, surnamed Shari'at-Madar, was an influential figure who tried to persuade the public to avoid violence, buried the bodies of martyred believers near the Masjid-i-Kazim-Big, and received a trust from Quddus.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Haná-Sab
people.bio: Muhammad-i-Hana-Sab was a resident of Isfahan who served in Baha'u'llah's home and informed him of Quddus's arrival at Shah-Rud, enabling Baha'u'llah to join him.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Karradi
people.bio: Haji Muhammad-i-Karradi of Baghdad was a courageous leader who fought against Ibrahim Pasha of Egypt and later became a fervent disciple of the Faith.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Khurasani
people.bio: Haji Mirza Muhammad-i-Khurasani was the leader of the Shaykhi community of Tehran and an instructor at the madrisih of Mirza Salih, who declined to respond when approached by Mulla Husayn.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Mamaqání
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-i-Mamaqani was a prominent and learned disciple of Shaykh Ahmad-i-Ahsa'i who was introduced to the new Revelation by Mulla Husayn in Tabriz and later participated in the tribunal of the Bab.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Mázandarání
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-i-Mazindarani was a survivor of the struggle of Tabarsi who, along with Shukru'llah-i-Nuri, accompanied Baha'u'llah on his journey to Karbila.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Mu
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-i-Mu'allim-i-Nuri was a native of Nur and a fervent admirer of Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kazim who introduced other seekers to the Faith and accompanied Baha'u'llah to Shah-Rud.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Túb-Chi
people.bio: The contexts mention Khan-Muhammad-i-Tub-Chi, a friend of Hujjat who petitioned to rescue the Bab, and Shaykh Muhammad-i-Tub-Chi, who was recognized as an able adversary by fleeing forces before being attacked and injured.

people.name: Muhammad-i-Zarí
people.bio: Karbila'i Mirza Muhammad-i-Zari' is mentioned in Chapter 22 of the narrative as one of the individuals in a listed group.

people.name: Muhít-i-Kirmání
people.bio: Mirza Muhit-i-Kirmani was a disciple of Siyyid Kazim who expressed readiness for a difficult mission, and later met the Bab during the latter's pilgrimage to Mecca, where he was directly challenged by the Bab's message.

people.name: Muhít-i-Shá'ir
people.bio: Mirza Muhit-i-Sha'ir-i-Kirmani was an extraordinarily lean and tall individual identified by some disciples of Siyyid Kazim as one of the vain and faithless people foretold by their master.

people.name: Músáy-i-Kázim
people.bio: Imam Musay-i-Kazim is a holy figure mentioned in Chapter 21 of the narrative, referenced by a guard who expressed remorse over being ordered to shed the blood of a holy person.

people.name: Músáy-i-Qumí
people.bio: Haji Mirza Musay-i-Qumi was a recipient of a letter from Baha'u'llah regarding restraint, who later advised a traveller in Qum to consult Azim in Kashan to find Mirza Ahmad.

people.name: Nabíl-i-Akbar
people.bio: Nabil-i-Akbar is mentioned in Chapter 19 as a witness to reports about Mulla Husayn, and in Chapter 20 as a relative of Mirza Muhammad-Baqir of Mashhad.

people.name: Navváb-i-Radaví
people.bio: Navvab-i-Radavi was a prominent adversary of Hujjat in Zanjan who maliciously criticized Hujjat's receptions and used public commotion to enlist the governor's support against him.

people.name: Niyáz-i-Baghdádí
people.bio: Haji Niyaz-i-Baghdadi was a pilgrim who traveled to a holy city in 1267 A.H. and was privileged to meet the Sherif.

people.name: Nubuvvat-i-Khássih
people.bio: In Chapter 10, Nubuvvat-i-Khassih is mentioned as a fundamental article of faith that the Bab was requested to expound and validate before an assemblage of accomplished divines in Isfahan.

people.name: Nuqtiy-i-Ukhrá
people.bio: In Chapter 20, Nuqtiy-i-Ukhra is mentioned as an unrivalled station, second only to that of the Bab Himself, which was conferred upon a believer in Baghdad during the revelation of the Kullu't-Ta'am verse.

people.name: Pay-i-Minár
people.bio: In Chapter 4, Pay-i-Minar is mentioned as a madrisih belonging to Mirza Salih in the capital where Mulla Husayn resided during his stay.

people.name: Prince Fírúz Mírzá
people.bio: Prince Firuz Mirza, the Nusratu'd-Dawlih, was the governor of Shiraz in Chapter 22 who ordered the prompt extermination of the occupants of the fort after a complete and sudden rout aroused his apprehensions.

people.name: Prince Mihdi
people.bio: Mihdi-Qulí Mírzá (referred to as Prince Mihdi) was a Qajar prince and royal military commander appointed by the Persian government to crush the Bábí uprising. He led the imperial troops besieging the Bábí defenders at Fort Shaykh Tabarsí. After suffering several humiliating defeats, he ultimately captured the fort through a treacherous oath of peace sworn on a copy of the Qur'án, which he violated by massacring the defenders and executing Quddús.

people.name: Prince Muhammad
people.bio: Prince Muhammad (referred to as Prince Muhammad-'Ali Mirza) was the eldest son of the Shah and governor of Kirmanshah in Chapter 1. He hosted Shaykh Ahmad in Kirmanshah, and his death freed Shaykh Ahmad from his solicitations to extend his stay.

people.name: Qabl-i-Muhammad
people.bio: In Chapter 8, Ali-Qabl-i-Muhammad is mentioned as a name added to the traditional formula of the adhan by a loyal believer, which enraptured Mulla Sadiq.

people.name: Qasídiy-i-Varqá
people.bio: In Chapter 1, Qasidiy-i-Varqa (referred to as Qasidiy-i-Varqa'iyyih) is mentioned as a work whose verses were shared by Haji Mirza Kamalu'd-Din-i-Naraqi to announce the Revelation of Baha'u'llah in Baghdad.

people.name: Qasim-i-Nayrizi
people.bio: Haji Qasim-i-Nayrizi was a companion who, along with Fathu'llah-i-Hakkak-i-Qumi, inflicted slight wounds on the Shah in Chapter 26. He was subjected to inhuman treatment and ultimately died from it.

people.name: Rahím-i-Khabbaz
people.bio: Mirza Rahim-i-Khabbaz was a believer in Chapter 8 who distinguished himself by his fearlessness and fiery ardour, relaxing not in his efforts until the hour of his death.

people.name: Rahím-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Mulla Rahim-i-Zanjani was one of the leading mujtahids of Zanjan, highly esteemed for his piety, learning, and force of character in Chapter 24. He was the father of Mulla Muhammad-'Ali, surnamed Hujjat.

people.name: Rajab-i-Haddad
people.bio: Mashhadi Rajab-i-Haddad is mentioned in Chapter 22 as the father of Muhammad, one of the companions listed in the narrative.

people.name: Rasul-i-Bahnimiri
people.bio: Rasul-i-Bahnimiri was a companion in the fort who complained to Quddus about intercepted bread and water in Chapter 19. In Chapter 20, driven by famine, he and a few others separated themselves from their friends.

people.name: Riday-i-Kuchik
people.bio: Ghulam-Riday-i-Kuchik was a companion of Vahid in Chapter 22 who distinguished himself in marksmanship and accompanied Vahid on his journey.

people.name: Riday-i-Manshadi
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-Riday-i-Manshadi was an enlightened ulama of Manshad who discarded his turban to serve as Vahid's doorkeeper in Chapter 22, and was ordered to sally forth to scatter a hostile crowd.

people.name: Riday-i-Pa-Qal
people.bio: Siyyid Muhammad-Riday-i-Pa-Qal'iyi was a distinguished divine and siyyid who recognized the truth of the Cause in Chapter 4. In Chapter 20, his declared purpose to enlist under Mulla Husayn's banner caused a great tumult among the ulamas of Isfahan.

people.name: Riday-i-Sardar
people.bio: Mir Riday-i-Sardar was a companion of Hujjat who, alongside Din-Muhammad-Vazir, secretly interred Hujjat's remains at midnight in Chapter 24 before the enemy could discover his death.

people.name: Riday-i-Sháh
people.bio: Mulla Riday-i-Shah was a native of Kirman and mason by profession who was slain two days after the abandonment of the fort by Quddus in the Panj-Shanbih-Bazar of Barfurush in Chapter 20.

people.name: Riday-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Ghulam-Riday-i-Yazdi was appointed captain of the forces by Vahid in Chapter 22 and was instructed to drive off the enemy. Another individual of the same name was listed as killed in the conflict.

people.name: Risaliy-i-Furu
people.bio: In Chapter 10, Risaliy-i-Furu (referred to as Risaliy-i-Furu'-i-'Adliyyih) is mentioned as a work of the Bab written in Arabic, which Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Harati undertook to translate into Persian.

people.name: Risaly-i-Sultaniyyih
people.bio: In Chapter 1, Risaly-i-Sultaniyyih is mentioned as an epistle written by Shaykh Ahmad to explain the Faith, which greatly pleased the Shah.

people.name: Sadiq-i-Khurasani
people.bio: Mulla Sadiq-i-Khurasani, also known as Muqaddas and surnamed Ismu'llahu'l-Asdaq, was a loyal believer who prepared the way in Isfahan. In Chapter 8, Quddus met him in Shiraz and entrusted him with the Khasa'il-i-Sab'ih to put its provisions into effect.

people.name: Sadiq-i-Tabrizi
people.bio: Sadiq-i-Tabrizi was a youth from Tihran who arrived at the house of the narrator's father in Chapter 21, lived in Qum in Chapter 23, and attempted to assassinate the Shah in Shimiran in Chapter 26.

people.name: Sadr-i-Ardibili
people.bio: Sadr-i-Ardibili was an arrogant and presumptuous ecclesiastical leader of Persia in Chapter 15. After attempting to beguile Muhammad Shah, he was denounced, banished from the capital, and condemned to obscurity in Qum.

people.name: Sahib-i-Divan
people.bio: In Chapter 8, Sahib-i-Divan (Mirza Fath-'Ali Khan) is mentioned in the narrative alongside Zillu's-Sultan as a party in a dispute whose verdict was to be determined by a Babi.

people.name: Sahifiyi-i-Baynu
people.bio: In Chapter 7, Sahifiyi-i-Baynu (referred to as Sahifiyi-i-Baynu'l-Haramayn) is mentioned as the Bab's written reply from Mecca to Medina addressing the questions of Mirza Muhit.

people.name: Sálih-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Haji Mulla Salih-i-Qazvini was the father of Tahirih and a respected mujtahid of Qazvin skilled in Muslim law in Chapter 3.

people.name: Sardar-i-Kull
people.bio: Sardar-i-Kull (Aziz Khan-i-Mukri) was a military officer who met Hujjat at Zanjan during a suspension of hostilities in Chapter 24.

people.name: Shafay-i-Masjid
people.bio: In Chapter 8, Shafay-i-Masjid (referred to as Madrisiy-i-Daru'sh-Shafay-i-Masjid-i-Shah) is mentioned as the madrisih in Tihran where the narrator met Siyyid Isma'il and Mulla Abdu'l-Karim.

people.name: Shahid-i-Thalith
people.bio: In Chapter 3, Shahid-i-Thalith is mentioned as the title given by the Shi'ahs to Mulla Muhammad, the son of Mulla Taqi, who was married to Tahirih.

people.name: Sharh-i-Khutbih
people.bio: In Chapter 2, Sharh-i-Khutbih is mentioned as a work of Siyyid Kazim whose introductory pages contain allusions to the name of Baha'u'llah.

people.name: Sharh-i-Qasidih
people.bio: In Chapter 2, Sharh-i-Qasidih is mentioned as a work of Siyyid Kazim whose introductory pages contain allusions to the name of Baha'u'llah.

people.name: Shaykh 'Abdu
people.bio: In Chapters 20 and 22, Shaykh Abdu is associated with Shaykh Abdu'l-Khaliq-i-Yazdi, whose son was a dedicated youth, and Haji Shaykh Abdu'l-'Ali, a judge and Vahid's father-in-law who joined in greeting him at Nayriz.

people.name: Shaykh 'Ábid
people.bio: Shaykh Abid, known as Shaykhuna, was a pious and learned tutor who was a disciple of Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kazim. In Chapter 3, he was entrusted with the education of the Bab and witnessed His superhuman wisdom.

people.name: Shaykh Abú
people.bio: Shaykh Abu (referred to as Shaykh Abu-Turab) was a disciple of Siyyid Kazim who secretly loved the Bab's Cause in Chapter 2, and later questioned the Bab regarding His Revelation in Chapter 8.

people.name: Shaykh 'Alí
people.bio: In the narrative, Shaykh Ali refers to multiple individuals, including Shaykh Ahmad's son who passed away the year the Bab was born, and Mulla Shaykh Ali (surnamed Azim), a prominent disciple mentioned in Chapter 6.

people.name: Shaykh 'Alí Mírzá
people.bio: Shaykh Ali Mirza (referred to as Shaykh Ali-Mirzay-i-Shirazi) was the nephew of the Imam-Jum'ih who recognized the Bab's mission in Chapter 8 and later suffered martyrdom in the dungeon where his Master was confined.

people.name: Shaykh 'Azím
people.bio: Shaykh Azim was a companion who lived with the narrator and Mirza Ahmad in Qum in Chapter 23, and insisted on opening a coffer containing a scroll written by the Bab.

people.name: Shaykh Hadi
people.bio: Shaykh Hadi, the son of Shaykh Muhsin, was appointed as the leader of a band directed to occupy and fortify the fort of Khajih near the Chinar-Sukhtih quarter in Chapter 22.

people.name: Shaykh Hasan
people.bio: Shaykh Hasan (referred to as Shaykh Hasan-i-Zunuzi) was a close associate of Siyyid Kazim who later accompanied the Bab to Shiraz and transcribed His Tablets in Chapter 9.

people.name: Shaykh Hasan-i-Zunúzí
people.bio: Shaykh Hasan-i-Zunuzi was a devoted early disciple and amanuensis of the Báb, serving Him during His confinement in the mountain fortress of Máh-Kú. The Báb prophesied to him that he would meet the Promised One ('Him Whom God shall make manifest') in Karbilá. In 1851, Bahá'u'lláh visited Karbilá and met Shaykh Hasan, intimating His station to him and fulfilling the Báb's prophecy. Shaykh Hasan lived his later years in Karbilá, writing and transcribing Tablets, and later suffered imprisonment during exiles, passing away in Kirmánsháh. His memoirs and reports serve as a valuable historical source for Nabíl's Narrative.

people.name: Shaykh Isma
people.bio: Shaykh Isma'il was a believer who discovered the isolated abode of Baha'u'llah and provided him and his companion with food in Chapter 25.

people.name: Shaykh Muhammad
people.bio: In Chapter 3, Shaykh Muhammad-Hasan's disciples joined adversaries to heap indignities upon Mulla Ali, while in Chapter 15, Shaykh Muhammad-i-Shibl, a prominent divine of Baghdad, was a staunch supporter of Tahirih.

people.name: Shaykh Muhammad-i-Shibl
people.bio: Shaykh Muhammad-i-Shibl was a prominent Arab Shaykhí scholar and leader in Baghdad who embraced the Bábí Faith and became a stalwart supporter of Táhirih during her stay in Iraq. He hosted her in his home, organized her theological discussions, and later financed and facilitated her journey to Persia. He passed away in Baghdad shortly after hearing of the martyrdom of the Báb.

people.name: Shaykh Muhammad-i-Túb
people.bio: Shaykh Muhammad-i-Tub-Chi was recognized as an able adversary by an affrighted band of believers in Chapter 24, who attacked and struck him on the head with an axe while he was unarmed.

people.name: Shaykh Muhsin
people.bio: Shaykh Muhsin is mentioned in Chapter 22 as the father of Shaykh Hadi, who was appointed to lead a band to occupy and fortify the fort of Khajih.

people.name: Shaykh Sálih
people.bio: Shaykh Salih was an Arab resident of Karbila who embraced the Cause through Tahirih's efforts in Chapter 15. He was arrested, falsely accused of murder by his enemies, and put to death, becoming the first martyr to shed his blood on Persian soil.

people.name: Shaykh Shahid-i-Mazkan
people.bio: Shaykh Shahid-i-Mazkan was a believer who recounted meeting Siyyid Basir during his passage through Qamsar in Chapter 25, describing the spiritual power Siyyid Basir had received from his intercourse with the Bab.

people.name: Shaykh Sultan
people.bio: Shaykh Sultan (referred to as Shaykh Sultan-i-Karbila'i) was a close companion of Siyyid Kazim who met the Bab and transcribed His Tablets. In Chapter 9, despite being ill, he received a special nocturnal visit from the Bab.

people.name: Shaykh Sultan-i-Karbila
people.bio: Shaykh Sultan-i-Karbila (referred to as Shaykh Sultan-i-Karbila'i) was an intimate companion of Siyyid Kazim and supporter of the Cause whose ancestors were prominent ulamas of Karbila in Chapter 9.

people.name: Shaykh Yusuf
people.bio: Shaykh Yusuf was a companion appointed by Vahid as the custodian of the funds for the fort's occupants in Chapter 22.

people.name: Shaykh-i-Kulayni
people.bio: Shaykh-i-Kulayni (Muhammad-ibn-i-Ya'qub) was the author of the Usul-i-Kafi whose shrine is located in Kulayn. In Chapter 12, Muhammad Big was ordered to escort the Bab to Kulayn and pitch a tent for Him near the village.

people.name: Shaykhi-i-Shivih-Kash
people.bio: Shaykhi-i-Shivih-Kash was a companion who was appointed to serve as the executioner for the forces defending the fort in Chapter 22.

people.name: Siyyid 'Abdu
people.bio: Siyyid Abdu (referred to as Siyyid Abdu'l-Baqi) was a well-known divine of Kashan and friend of Mulla Husayn. Although he met the Bab in Chapter 11 and heard the verses He revealed, he ultimately failed to be moved by them and refused to believe in the truth of the Cause.

people.name: Siyyid 'Ábid
people.bio: Haji Siyyid Abid was a companion who accompanied Vahid to the enemy's camp in Chapter 22. He acted treacherously by communicating Vahid's secret instructions to Zaynu'l-'Abidin Khan instead of delivering them to the fort's occupants.

people.name: Siyyid Abú
people.bio: Siyyid Abu (referred to as Siyyid Abu-Talib or Siyyid Abu-Talib-i-Sang-Sari) was one of the survivors of the siege who related the circumstances of the martyrdoms to Baha'u'llah. He survived delivery to Mulla Zaynu'l-'Abidin and active service to the Cause in Mazindaran.

people.name: Siyyid Ahmad
people.bio: Siyyid Ahmad was a companion of Quddus and resident of Sang-Sar who, along with his brother Mir Abu'l-Qasim, was foretold in a dream to attain the presence of the promised Qa'im and suffer martyrdom. He departed for Karbila with his father and brother to meet Siyyid Kazim, and was later ushered into the presence of the prince following the siege of Fort Tabarsi.

people.name: Siyyid 'Alí Khan
people.bio: Siyyid Ali Khan served as host to Aziz Khan in Zanjan, facilitating a meeting between Aziz Khan and Hujjat. He was subsequently arrested by the farrash of the Amir-Nizam to be conducted to the capital after conveying Hujjat's intentions to Aziz Khan.

people.name: Siyyid 'Alíy-i-Tafarshi
people.bio: Mirza Siyyid Aliy-i-Tafarshi, surnamed the Majdu'l-Ashraf, was a figure to whom copies of a text were presented, though he expressed views of a totally different character from Mustawfiyu'l-Mamalik-i-Ashtiyani, who had been won over to the Faith.

people.name: Siyyid 'Alíy-i-Zargar
people.bio: Siyyid Aliy-i-Zargar was a companion who stole out of the fort at midnight carrying a share of rice received from Quddus. He delivered a sealed letter to Abbas-Quli Khan, with whom he was already acquainted.

people.name: Siyyid Asadu
people.bio: Haji Siyyid Asadu'llah (referred to as Siyyid Asadu) was a religious figure whose disciples complained to him about Mulla Husayn's preaching in Isfahan. He returned evasive answers, prompting his disconcerted disciples to refer the matter to Haji Muhammad-Ibrahim-i-Kalbasi and the governor.

people.name: Siyyid Ashraf
people.bio: Siyyid Ashraf was a martyr of the Faith from Zanjan and the son of Mir Jalil. The author was closely acquainted with him and drew upon his testimony for the account of the happenings of Zanjan.

people.name: Siyyid Hasan
people.bio: Siyyid Hasan is mentioned as the brother of Siyyid Husayn-i-Yazdi, selected by the Bab to accompany Him during His sojourn in the province of Adhirbayjan. The text also mentions a Mirza Siyyid Hasan whose sister was united in wedlock with the Bab.

people.name: Siyyid Husayn-i-Azghandi
people.bio: Siyyid Husayn-i-Azghandi was the foremost mujtahid of Yazd who welcomed his nephew to the city after sending him a written request to hasten to Yazd and deliver him from the machinations of Haji Mirza Aqasi.

people.name: Siyyid Husayn-i-Kulah
people.bio: Siyyid Husayn-i-Kulah-Duz (referred to as Siyyid Husayn-i-Kulah) was a resident of Barfurush whose head was impaled on a lance and paraded in the narrative of Chapter 20.

people.name: Siyyid Husayn-i-Yazdi
people.bio: Siyyid Husayn-i-Yazdi, known by his title 'Azíz', was one of the early Letters of the Living and the trusted personal amanuensis (secretary) of the Báb. He accompanied the Báb during His long imprisonments in the fortresses of Máh-Kú and Chihríq, transcribing many of His major works and letters. During the Báb's execution in Tabríz, Siyyid Husayn was confined in the same cell and initially recanted his faith under the Báb's instruction to save and preserve the sacred writings. He was subsequently taken to Tehran, where he was imprisoned in the Siyáh-Chál and martyred during the persecutions of 1852.

people.name: Siyyid Husayn-i-Zavari
people.bio: Siyyid Husayn-i-Zavari'i (referred to as Siyyid Husayn-i-Zavari) visited Zarand, where he befriended the narrator and shared a copy of the 'Risaliy-i-Adliyyih' with him, allaying his agitation and enlightening him on the Faith.

people.name: Siyyid Ibráhím
people.bio: Siyyid Ibrahim is mentioned in the narrative in two capacities: as Siyyid Ibrahim-i-Qazvini, a notorious Shi'ah leader and enemy of Siyyid Kazim who incited opposition against him, and as Siyyid Ibrahim-i-Khalil, a companion in Tabriz entrusted with the Bab's commentaries.

people.name: Siyyid Ibráhím-i-Khalil
people.bio: Siyyid Ibrahim-i-Khalil was a resident of Tabriz who was entrusted with concealing and preserving the Bab's commentaries and Tablets revealed in Mah-Ku and Chihriq. He was also informed by Azim of the Bab's approaching arrival in Tabriz.

people.name: Siyyid Ibráhím-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Siyyid Ibrahim-i-Qazvini was a powerful and notorious Shi'ah leader who instigated the enemies of Siyyid Kazim to league together and attempt to destroy him.

people.name: Siyyid Isma
people.bio: Siyyid Isma'il-i-Zavari'i (referred to as Siyyid Isma), surnamed Dhabih, was a cousin of Siyyid Husayn who met the Bab in Isfahan and later introduced the narrator to Mulla Abdu'l-Karim. He recognized the new Revelation, introduced the narrator to it, and eventually offered his life as a sacrifice in Baghdad in the path of Baha'u'llah.

people.name: Siyyid Javád-i-Kirmání
people.bio: Haji Siyyid Javad-i-Kirmani was a scholar of Kirman known for his skill and competence. He received Quddus, an old acquaintance from Karbila, at his home upon his arrival.

people.name: Siyyid Kázim-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Siyyid Kazim-i-Zanjani was a devoted early Bábí who accompanied the Báb on His journey from Shiraz to Isfahan in 1846, delivering the Báb's letter to the governor Manuchihr Khán. He was renowned for his theological knowledge and defense of the Faith. He was later martyred during the Battle of Fort Shaykh Tabarsí in Mazindaran, and his brother Siyyid Murtadá was one of the Seven Martyrs of Tehran.

people.name: Siyyid Mihdi
people.bio: Siyyid Mihdi is mentioned in Chapter 22 as one of the sons who, along with Siyyid Ahmad, accompanied his father on a journey from Yazd, leaving his other brothers in the care of their mother.

people.name: Siyyid Mírzá Husayn-i-Mutavalli
people.bio: Siyyid Mirza Husayn-i-Mutavalli was a defender of the fort of Tabarsi who, accompanied by his servant, left the fort to join the prince in his camp during the siege.

people.name: Siyyid Muhammad
people.bio: Haji Siyyid Muhammad-Baqir-i-Rashti (referred to as Siyyid Muhammad) was a renowned ecclesiastical dignitary of Isfahan. Mulla Husayn, acting on Siyyid Kazim's instructions, presented the Faith to him, deeply impressing him with his poise and confidence.

people.name: Siyyid Muhammad Báqir-i-Rashtí
people.bio: Hájí Siyyid Muhammad Báqir-i-Rashtí was a prominent and highly influential ecclesiastical leader (mujtahid) residing in Isfahan. Although initially critical of the Shaykhí teachings of Shaykh Ahmad and Siyyid Kázim, Siyyid Kázim sent Mullá Husayn on a special mission to Isfahan to meet him. Mullá Husayn successfully resolved his theological objections, leading Siyyid Muhammad Báqir to write a warm letter of endorsement and support, which greatly strengthened Siyyid Kázim's standing and protected the early community.

people.name: Siyyid Muhammad-i-Gulpaygani
people.bio: Siyyid Muhammad-i-Gulpaygani, whose pen-name was Ta'ir and whom Tahirih styled Fata'l-Malih, was one of Tahirih's companions in Persia who was bidden by her to return home.

people.name: Siyyid Muhammad-i-Isfahání
people.bio: Siyyid Muhammad-i-Isfahani is mentioned in Chapter 19 as one of the companions associated with the events in Khurasan.

people.name: Siyyid Muhsin
people.bio: Mirza Siyyid Muhsin was a notable of Tabriz who converted to the Faith after hearing the account of the Bab's martyrdom. He later met the narrator and conducted him to the scene of the martyrdom, showing him where the Bab was suspended and the room where He had conversed with Siyyid Husayn.

people.name: Siyyid Murtada
people.bio: Siyyid Murtada was one of the Seven Martyrs of Tihran. Quddus cited him in a discussion as a descendant of the Prophet who was highly esteemed by the ulamas.

people.name: Siyyid Riday-i-Khurasani
people.bio: Siyyid Riday-i-Khurasani was chosen alongside Mulla Yusuf-i-Ardibili to act as a representative for the besieged at Fort Tabarsi, meeting with Mihdi-Quli Mirza to convey their readiness for peaceful negotiations.

people.name: Siyyid-i-Khal-Dar
people.bio: Siyyid Abdu'l-'Azim-i-Khu'i, surnamed Siyyid-i-Khal-Dar, participated in the defense of Fort Tabarsi and was known for his dignified bearing. He was commissioned by Vahid to ride through the streets and publicly proclaim a warning to their attackers.

people.name: Siyyid-i-Nahri
people.bio: Siyyid-i-Nahri, along with his brother Mirza Hadi, sought refuge in a deserted castle but was advised that it was unnecessary to desert their companions.

people.name: Siyyid-i-Nur
