people.bio: Haji Siyyid Javad was known as the Siyyid-i-Nur due to his kindly disposition, extreme forbearance, and simplicity. He traveled to Iraq and Khurasan, and once encountered the Shah in Tihran without being disturbed by his presence.

people.name: Siyyid-i-Qumí
people.bio: The Siyyid-i-Qumi was an individual who treacherously deserted Fort Tabarsi. During Quddus's martyrdom, he smote him in the face and mocked his claims.

people.name: Sulayman-i-Khatib
people.bio: Mirza Sulayman-i-Khatib (referred to as Sulayman-i-Khatib) was a believer who was entrusted by the Bab with a Tablet revealed near the shrine of Shah 'Abdu'l-'Azim. He was instructed to go to the shrine with other believers and chant it there.

people.name: Sulayman-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Sulayman-i-Nuri (referred to as Sulayman-i-Nuri) was a believer who informed the narrator of Tahirih's escape from Qazvin and her journey toward Khurasan. He also chanted a Tablet revealed by Baha'u'llah in the presence of the assembled believers.

people.name: Súriy-i-Ayyúb
people.bio: The 'Suriy-i-Ayyub' is a Tablet revealed by Baha'u'llah in which Haji Muhammad-Taqi is mentioned.

people.name: Tabib-i-Zanjani
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-Aliy-i-Tabib-i-Zanjani (referred to as Tabib-i-Zanjani) was a martyr of the Faith from Zanjan whose testimony provided a primary account of the events there. He was known for his tenacity of faith and devotion, and later helped direct the narrator to Mirza Ahmad in Hamadan.

people.name: Táhir-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Mulla Tahir-i-Shirazi (referred to as Tahir-i-Shirazi) was a companion esteemed for his learning and character who was savagely put to death upon his arrival at Qazvin.

people.name: Tálib-i-Sang-Sarí
people.bio: Siyyid Abu-Talib-i-Sang-Sari (referred to as Talib-i-Sang-Sari) was a survivor of the siege of Fort Tabarsi who related the circumstances of his brothers' martyrdoms in a communication to Baha'u'llah.

people.name: Tálib-i-Shahmirzadi
people.bio: Siyyid Abu-Talib-i-Shahmirzadi (referred to as Talib-i-Shahmirzadi) wrote an account of the struggle of Mazindaran that was sent to the Holy Land.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Harati
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Harati (referred to as Taqiy-i-Harati) was an eminent mujtahid who praised the Bab's writings and met Him daily at the residence of the Imam-Jum'ih. With the Bab's consent, he translated the Arabic work 'Risaliy-i-Furu'-i-'Adliyyih' into Persian.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Juvayní
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Juvayni (referred to as Taqiy-i-Juvayni), a Sabzivar native of literary accomplishments and courage, served as a counsellor to Quddus and was entrusted by Mulla Husayn to command encounters and lead charges. He was eventually martyred in the path of the Faith.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Juvayníy
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Juvayniy-i-Sabzivari (referred to as Taqiy-i-juvayniy) was a man of literary accomplishments and fearless courage who requested to take the qalyan to Khusraw during the events described in Chapter 19.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Kirmání
people.bio: Haji Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Kirmani (referred to as Taqiy-i-Kirmani) was a prominent merchant of Kirman who established a business branch in Khurasan. He met an old acquaintance in Kazimayn and was later executed, expressing violent indignation at the sight of the executioner.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Milani
people.bio: Haji Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Milani (referred to as Taqiy-i-Milani) was a noted merchant of Tabriz who, along with Haji Ali-'Askar, risked arrest to visit the Bab. They were admitted after the Bab sent a message ordering the guards to let them enter.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Núrí
people.bio: Mirza Muhammad Taqiy-i-Nuri (referred to as Taqiy-i-Nuri) was a celebrated mujtahid in the district of Nur whose authority and influence were highly eminent prior to the declaration of the Bab.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Qarakhili
people.bio: Mulla Muhammad-Taqiy-i-Qarakhili (referred to as Taqiy-i-Qarakhili) is mentioned in Chapter 20 of the narrative alongside other companions and residents who participated in the events.

people.name: Taqíy-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Mulla Taqiy-i-Qazvini (referred to as Taqiy-i-Qazvini) was a figure whose slaying led to the narrator's first imprisonment due to the assistance extended to those who were severely and undeservedly punished as a result.

people.name: Táríkh-i-Nasiri
people.bio: The 'Tarikh-i-Nasiri' is a historical work by Rida-Quli Khan-i-Lalih-Bashi in which the author lavished praise on the strength and skill of Mulla Husayn's stroke.

people.name: Umm-i-Ashraf
people.bio: Umm-i-Ashraf was a newly married woman who distinguished herself by the tenacity of her faith during the Zanjan conflict. She gave birth to her son Ashraf inside the fort, and both mother and child survived the final massacre.

people.name: Umm-i-Salmih
people.bio: Fatimih, surnamed Umm-i-Salmih and designated as Zakiyyih, was born in 1233 A.H., the same year as Baha'u'llah. She suffered martyrdom at the age of thirty-six.

people.name: Usúl-i-Káfí
people.bio: The 'Usul-i-Kafi' is a book authored by Shaykh-i-Kulayni (Muhammad-ibn-i-Ya'qub) that is cited as a source of authority and tradition to support religious arguments.

people.name: Vahhab-i-Qazvini
people.bio: Mulla Abdu'l-Vahhab-i-Qazvini (referred to as Vahhab-i-Qazvini) was the father of Mirza Hadi, who attained the presence of the Bab.

people.name: Vahhab-i-Shirazi
people.bio: Mirza Abdu'l-Vahhab-i-Shirazi (referred to as Vahhab-i-Shirazi), son of Haji Abdu'l-Majid, owned a shop in Karbila and left all his possessions to follow Baha'u'llah. He was subsequently arrested, imprisoned in chains with Baha'u'llah in Tihran, and eventually suffered martyrdom.

people.name: Vahhab-i-Turshizi
people.bio: Mirza Abdu'l-Vahhab-i-Turshizi (referred to as Vahhab-i-Turshizi) was a believer living in Khuy who was instructed to proceed immediately to Urumiyyih, where the Bab promised to join him.

people.name: Yahyáy-i-Darabi
people.bio: Siyyid Yahyay-i-Darabi (referred to as Yahyay-i-Darabi) was an eloquent, learned, and highly esteemed subject delegated by the Shah to investigate the Bab's claims. He subsequently embraced the Cause and became a Babi, prompting imperial orders that forbade anyone to detract from his exalted rank.

people.name: Yúsuf-i-Ardibili
people.bio: Mulla Yusif-i-Ardibili (referred to as Yusif-i-Ardibili) was a disciple who attained the presence of the Bab and was personally invested by Him.

people.name: Zakariyyay-i-Qádí-Kalá
people.bio: Zakariyyay-i-Qadi-Kala'i (referred to as Zakariyyay-i-Qadi-Kala) was a cousin of Khusraw and a valiant officer of the opposing army. He was killed while defending a barricade that was charged and broken by a Babi defender.

people.name: Ziyárat-i-Sháh
people.bio: The 'Ziyarat-i-Shah-'Abdu'l-'Azim' is a Tablet revealed by the Bab near the shrine of Shah 'Abdu'l-'Azim, expressing His wish regarding the eventual transfer of bodies.

events.title: Declaration of the Báb
events.description: On May 23, 1844, Siyyid 'Alí-Muhammad declared His mission as the Báb to Mullá Husayn in Shiraz, marking the birth of the Bábí Faith.

events.title: Arrest and Scourging of Mullá Sádiq
events.description: Mullá Sádiq-i-Muqaddas was arrested and publicly scourged in Shiraz for adding the Báb's name to the call to prayer.

events.title: House Arrest of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was arrested upon His return from pilgrimage and placed under house arrest at the home of His uncle, Khál-i-A'zam.

events.title: Unveiling at Badasht Preparation
events.description: Tehran served as the assembly point for many early Bábís, where Bahá'u'lláh organized the Bábís and prepared them for the conference.

events.title: Martyrdom of the Seven Martyrs
events.description: Seven prominent Bábís, including the Báb's uncle Khál-i-A'zam, were publicly executed in Tehran for refusing to recant their faith.

events.title: Imprisonment in the Siyáh-Chál
events.description: Following the attempt on the Shah's life in 1852, Bahá'u'lláh and other Bábís were cast into the dark dungeon, where Bahá'u'lláh received His divine revelation.

events.title: Proclamation by Vahíd
events.description: Vahíd arrived in Yazd, attracting thousands to the Báb's cause and hosting massive religious debates.

events.title: Anti-Bábí Riots
events.description: The local clergy incited mobs to attack Vahíd and his hosts, resulting in the martyrdom of several early believers and forcing Vahíd to flee to Nayríz.

events.title: Trial of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was brought from His fortress prison to stand trial before a tribunal of Shia clergy and Crown Prince Násiri'd-Dín Mírzá, where He declared His station.

events.title: Martyrdom of the Báb
events.description: On July 9, 1850, the Báb and His disciple Anís were executed in the public square of Tabriz by a firing squad.

events.title: Refuge under Manúchihr Khán
events.description: The Báb was hosted in Isfahan by the sympathetic governor Manúchihr Khán, who offered Him protection and his entire fortune to spread the Faith.

events.title: Governor's Death and Arrest
events.description: Upon the governor's death in 1847, the Báb was arrested by order of the Grand Vizier and escorted to Tehran and then Máh-Kú.

events.title: Conversion of Hujjat
events.description: Hujjat converted to the Bábí Faith, leading his entire congregation of several thousand to accept the Báb.

events.title: The Zanján Upheaval
events.description: A brutal siege (1850-1851) where the Bábí quarter, led by Hujjat, defended itself against imperial troops before being massacred.

events.title: Arrival of Vahíd
events.description: Vahíd arrived in Nayriz, leading to the conversion of the local governor's family and thousands of townspeople.

events.title: Nayríz Upheavals
events.description: A series of battles at the fort of Khájih where Vahíd and the Bábís defended themselves before being betrayed by a false treaty.

events.title: Conference of Badasht
events.description: A historic gathering of 81 Bábís in 1848, organized by Bahá'u'lláh, where Táhirih appeared unveiled, signaling the independence of the new dispensation.

events.title: Imprisonment of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was imprisoned in the castle of Maku, where He composed the Bayán, His primary doctrinal work.

events.title: Conversion of warden 'Alí Khán
events.description: The warden 'Alí Khán fell under the Báb's influence and relaxed the prison restrictions, allowing pilgrims to reach the Báb.

events.title: Confinement of the Báb
events.description: The Báb was transferred to Chihríq fortress to isolate Him further from His followers, but the warden also became sympathetic.

events.title: Shaykhí Lectures
events.description: Siyyid Kázim taught the imminent advent of the Mihdí, making Karbila the focal point of spiritual search.

events.title: Arrest of Bastámí
events.description: Mullá 'Alíy-i-Bastámí arrived to announce the Báb's declaration, leading to his arrest and the start of official persecutions.

events.title: Shaykh Ahmad debates
events.description: Shaykh Ahmad resided and taught in Najaf, establishing his intellectual authority.

events.title: Arrest and Martyrdom of Quddús
events.description: Quddús was brutally tortured and martyred in the public square of his home city by an incited mob led by local clerics.

events.title: Birthplace of Mullá Husayn
events.description: The hometown of Mullá Husayn, from which he set out to find the Promised One.

events.title: Murder of Mullá Taqí
events.description: The uncle and father-in-law of Táhirih was murdered in the mosque, sparking massive arrests of Bábís.

events.title: House arrest of Táhirih
events.description: Táhirih was imprisoned in her family home before being secretly rescued by agents of Bahá'u'lláh and brought to Tehran.

events.title: Raising the Black Standard
events.description: Mullá Husayn raised the Black Standard of Khurasan in Mashhad, marching with Bábí defenders toward Mazandaran.
